bull_thesis: Prosegur is a three-engine physical-security conglomerate trading at 5.5x TTM FCF and ~4.9% dividend yield while every operating engine is inflecting up. The load-bearing facts are hard, not narrative: net income +52.8% (EUR78M->EUR119M FY2025), EPS +46.7% (EUR0.15->EUR0.22), EBITA +8.9% to EUR357M, and TTM FCF EUR218M (+53% YoY) against FLAT reported revenue (+0.5%) -- pure operating leverage plus tax-rate normalization (effective tax 42.0%, -449bps YoY), not a demand sugar-high. Reported growth is depressed by ARS/USD translation; organic growth is +10% FY2025 / +7.8% Q1-2026. Three engines, three re-rate paths: (1) CASH -- the ~74%-owned listed Prosegur Cash (CASH.MC) is a Brink's-grade CIT franchise at 11.3% EBITA margin with mix-shift to recurring 'transformation products' now 36.4% of Cash sales; the market already values Cash at 8.9x EBITDA / EUR1.83B EV. (2) SECURITY -- EUR2.7B revenue, crossed Q1 cash-flow breakeven 'for the first time in a first quarter', US double-digit, Cipher cyber folded in to reposition as integrated physical+digital security. (3) ALARMS -- a real subscription compounder: base +8.9% to 1.07M, churn cut 95->67bps, service margin 21%->25%, activation-fee revenue +35%. Deleveraging on track (2.4x, -EUR28M QoQ, BBB, 60%+ fixed at 2.9%). Guidance credible (5/7 beats, FY2025 beat its own target, point Q2/Q3 guides EUR1.44B/EUR1.49B). At 5.5x FCF you are paid to own a deleveraging, earnings-inflecting cash machine alongside an aligned controlling family. If FCF holds at EUR218M and the market pays even 9-10x (a discount to Securitas ~12-14x), equity is worth EUR1.96-2.18B vs ~EUR1.45B today -- a 35-50% re-rate while collecting ~4.9%.
bear_thesis: Prosegur is a structurally low-quality, FX-cursed, low-margin manned-guarding conglomerate that DESERVES its discount and has NO catalyst to close it. Net margin 2.4%, ROIC 3.7%, Security EBITA margin just 2.8% -- a labor-arbitrage guard business with razor-thin economics; no amount of 'integrated cyber' branding earns a tech multiple on 2.8% margins. The growth story is entirely 'organic'; in reported euros -- the only currency shareholders are paid in -- revenue is FLAT (+0.5% FY, +1.5% Q1) because ARS/USD repeatedly eat it. Argentina is a recurring, uncontrollable wound: Cash EBITA was 'negatively impacted by Argentina macroeconomic conditions' and one more peso devaluation flattens the reported line again. The EPS inflection is lower-quality than it looks -- much is tax normalization and operating leverage off a depressed base -- and management conceded margin gains 'will be less significant over the course of the year'. Leverage is heavy: EUR1.62B net debt vs EUR119M net income; deleveraging at ~EUR28M/quarter is glacial. Most damning for a deep-value thesis: NO forcing function. The only structural event -- Gubel's tender -- closed March 2024 at EUR1.83/share, BELOW today's EUR2.68, and Gubel already controls ~75%, so the cheap re-rate event already fired in the wrong direction and minorities are at the mercy of a controlling family with no obligation to surface value, no announced buyback ramp, no Cash take-private, no asset sale, no activist (insider buy ratio 0.00). A listed parent owning a listed sub is a permanent SoP-discount machine that rarely closes without an unannounced corporate action. The stock stays a 5.5x-FCF value trap; you collect 4.9% to wait indefinitely.
consensus_delta: For a deep_value name the question is whether the street prices terminal decline into stable/growing earnings power. Here the street is NOT badly offside -- it prices a deserved quality discount and is roughly right on direction if not magnitude. The genuine mispricing the consensus underweights: the QUALITY of the FY2025 earnings inflection (+52.8% net income, +53% FCF) is real and durable (operating leverage + permanent tax-rate normalization + Alarms subscription mix-shift), not a one-off, yet the stock still trades at 5.5x FCF as if FCF were peak/cyclical. The street's error is treating consolidated EUR218M FCF as fragile when ~36% of Cash is now recurring 'transformation products' and Alarms churn has structurally fallen 95->67bps. Where the street is RIGHT (and the bull must concede): reported revenue is flat, ARS is a recurring tax on the reported line, and there is no catalyst -- so the discount can persist for years. The expectations arbitrage is modest, not violent: a quality-of-earnings re-rate from 5.5x toward 8x FCF, not a special-situation re-rate to fair value.
forcing_function: There is NO hard-dated forcing function -- the thesis's central weakness. The next discrete events are ordinary earnings prints (Q2-2026 in ~July/August, screener ~48 days to next earnings) which can confirm the FCF/margin trajectory but are not re-rating catalysts. A genuine forcing function would require an UNANNOUNCED corporate action: a Prosegur Cash take-private/squeeze-out (Gubel already controls the parent; consolidating the listed Cash sub would collapse the holdco discount), a sizable buyback authorization, an asset sale of the Cash or Alarms stake, or activist entry -- none on the calendar as of 2026-06-13. Absent that, the re-rate depends on slow FCF-multiple convergence over multiple quarters. Honest answer for the director: WATCH the next 1-2 prints for FX-clean FCF confirmation and any capital-allocation pivot (buyback/Cash consolidation); enter on a flush, not on the catalyst, because the catalyst does not exist yet.
valley_of_death: The dominant 3-9 month risk is an ARS (secondarily USD) devaluation that flattens the reported P&L again -- Cash EBITA was already 'negatively impacted by Argentina macroeconomic conditions' in Q1-2026, and a sharp peso move would erase the reported growth narrative and likely the stock's recent strength even as organic numbers stay fine. Second, Security margin disappointment: management already guided that margin gains 'will be less significant over the course of the year', so a Q2/Q3 print showing the 2.8% EBITA margin stalling would puncture the 'structural margin transformation' thesis. Third, deleveraging is slow (~EUR28M/quarter) against EUR1.62B net debt; any working-capital reversal (the Q1 swing to +EUR8M operating cash flow from -EUR19M is seasonally fragile) could stall the debt-reduction story underpinning the equity-compounding case. No debt maturity wall (BBB, 60%+ fixed, EUR60M EIB loan just secured, EUR600M Cash bond already amortized), so the risk is earnings/FX, not refinancing. No cash-burn hump -- the business is FCF-positive.
positioning_washout: Limited mechanical-selling dynamics. The dividend (~EUR0.13/sh, ~4.9% yield, ~77% payout) is stable and uncut, so there is no yield-base liquidation trigger of the kind that washes out a dividend-cutter. The more relevant fact is the OPPOSITE of a washout: Gubel/Revoredo controls ~75%, so free float is thin (~25%) and illiquid -- which both caps forced selling AND means there is no large mechanical buyer to drive a re-rate. No index-inclusion catalyst, no forced-seller setup, no activist accumulation visible (insider buy ratio 0.00). The thin-float, controlled-company structure is double-edged: it floors downside (no big holder to dump) but removes any positioning-driven forcing function on the upside.
moderator_conclusion: PSG.MC is a high-quality forensic READ on a low-quality business with real but slow-burn cheapness and NO catalyst. The Interrogator scored it 4/5 credibility (STRENGTHENING) -- earnings +52.8%, FCF +53%, Alarms economics, deleveraging all genuinely improving, management confident and granular on what it controls. The Architect's SoP brackets EUR1.90 (strict parts, FX-impaired) to EUR3.60 (FCF-anchored bull); the reconciled base is ~EUR2.85, a thin ~6% premium to EUR2.68 once the deserved thin-margin (2.8% Security EBITA, 3.7% ROIC) and holdco/FX discounts are respected -- the screener's +75-81% Graham/IV15 MoS is optimistic. Applying the REGIME (CATALYST_WATCH_REGIME.md): the regime rewards hard-dated structural special-sits in thin-coverage distressed/deleveraging/forced-seller lanes and explicitly DEPRIORITIZES theses with no dated forcing function or that rely on slow multiple convergence. PSG has FIRED its only catalyst (Gubel tender, 2024, at EUR1.83 below today's price), Gubel already owns ~75% (thin float, no mechanical re-rate buyer), and there is no announced buyback/take-private/asset-sale -- so on the CATALYST axis the regime moves this DOWN. Verdict B (watchlist), conviction 2: do not chase at EUR2.68; the right entry is a capitulation flush (an ARS-devaluation-driven reported-revenue scare that knocks the price toward bear-SoP ~EUR1.90), where you would buy a deleveraging EUR218M-FCF machine at ~4x FCF with a ~6% yield. Separately, value_conviction 4: stripping out catalyst and regime, the VALUE case is strong -- a genuinely cheap (5.5x FCF), genuinely inflecting (+53% FCF/+53% NI), well-financed (BBB) franchise with high forensic credibility (4/5) trading near or below a defensible blended SoP; the only thing capping value_conviction at 4 not 5 is the structurally low ROIC/margin and FX fragility that make the discount partly deserved. The two scores diverge deliberately: cheap and clean (value 4), but no catalyst and out-of-favor lane (conviction 2).
sop_breakdown: Base case blends the FCF-anchored consolidated value (which captures the real EUR218M cash machine) against the strict segment-EBITA-minus-leverage parts (which respects the deserved thin-margin/holdco discount), then haircuts for FX and the absence of a catalyst. Parts at base multiples: CASH 74% stake ~EUR0.78B (between intrinsic EUR0.84B and market EUR0.72B); SECURITY ~EUR0.54B (7x ~EUR77M EBITA); ALARMS ~EUR0.36B (1.4x sales). Gross ~EUR1.68B less parent net debt EUR0.76B = EUR0.92B -> ~EUR1.70/sh on disaggregated parts; the consolidated FCF lens (EUR218M x ~8x = EUR1.74B equity -> EUR3.23/sh) pulls fair value up because Prosegur's value is in aggregate cash generation, not separable segments. Weighting the consolidated cash-machine view ~60% and strict parts ~40%, with a modest FX/holdco haircut, lands base-case fair value at EUR2.85/sh -- a slim ~6% premium to EUR2.68. Cheap, but the deserved discount eats most of the headline 75-81% model MoS.
risk_reward: Thin margin of safety, NOT asymmetric. Downside-to-break (bear SoP) EUR1.90 = -29% from EUR2.68; upside-to-base-fair EUR2.85 = +6%; upside-to-bull EUR3.60 = +34%. Base-case R:R ~0.2:1 (poor); bull-case R:R ~1.2:1. The screener's +75-81% Graham/IV15 MoS overstates the edge -- once the thin-margin/holdco/FX discount is respected, realistic upside-to-fair is single-digit at base and ~34% only in the no-FX-shock bull. A 'cheap with a thin margin of safety' name, not an asymmetric special-sit.
catalyst_status: FIRED. The only structural catalyst -- the Gubel, S.L. (Revoredo family) tender offer -- was authorized by CNMV on 2024-02-20 and completed 2024-03-18 at EUR1.83/share for ~13.23% of capital, lifting Gubel to ~74.8% ownership. That event is >2 years old AND priced BELOW today's EUR2.68, so the re-rate it could have driven is spent and went the wrong way for minorities. Verified as of 2026-06-13: NO live tender/squeeze-out, NO announced buyback ramp, NO Prosegur Cash take-private/delisting, NO asset sale, NO activist (insider buy ratio 0.00); the Q1-2026 call disclosed zero capital-action plans beyond routine deleveraging. The thesis rests entirely on FCF-multiple convergence with no dated forcing function.
peer_comps_note: Per peer_groups: true comps are global contract-security firms (Securitas SECU-B.ST, G4S/Allied Universal private) plus its own listed Cash sub (Prosegur Cash CASH.MC) and Brink's (BCO) for cash logistics. At 5.5x P/FCF, PSG screens cheap vs Securitas (~12-14x FCF) -- BUT ROIC 3.7% and net margin 2.4% mean the discount is partly EARNED (LatAm FX drag + structurally low-margin manned guarding). Sanity-check on the SoP multiple: the listed Cash sub trades at 8.9x EBITDA / EUR1.83B EV (vs Brink's ~6.5x), so the crown-jewel segment is NOT cheap on its own quote -- it is the consolidated holdco that carries the discount. Verdict cheap_vs_peers holds on FCF, but quality-adjusted the gap to Securitas is justified, not pure mispricing.
interrogator_dossier:
# Prosegur Compañía de Seguridad, S.A. (PSG.MC) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator dossier (Claude Opus 4.8). Source: Prosegur FY2025 results & press release (prosegur.com); Q4-2025 and Q1-2026 earnings calls/slides (Investing.com transcripts); company IR; CNMV history (Gubel tender offer). No FMP transcript was bundled; this dossier reasons from the screener financial trajectory cross-referenced against the most recent management commentary found on the web. Quotes are management phrasings reported in the Q1-2026 / Q4-2025 call coverage; numbers from the screener block are labeled (financials). Latest hard quarter: Q1 FY2026 (ended 31-Mar-2026).*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Prosegur is a three-engine physical-security conglomerate — **Security** (manned guarding + tech), **Cash** (cash-in-transit and cash management, held via the ~74% owned listed subsidiary Prosegur Cash, CASH.MC), and **Alarms** (residential/SME monitoring). The story management has told across the last several quarters is consistent and, unusually for a deep-value name, *improving*: a multi-year "value creation for shareholders" deleveraging-and-margin-recovery arc.

The arc's evolution: the older narrative was dominated by **LatAm FX as an existential drag** masking real volume growth. By FY2025 the framing shifted to "**organic growth is the truth, FX is noise**" — management leads every release with organic figures (FY2025 organic +10%, Q1-2026 organic +7.8%) and treats the reported number (FY2025 +0.5%, Q1-2026 +1.5%) as a translation artifact. The newest thread, introduced in Q1-2026, is **structural margin transformation**: in Cash, "transformation products now represent 36.4% of total Cash sales" (recurring, higher-margin services replacing volume-linked CIT); in Alarms, a deliberate pivot from "grow the base at any cost" to "**quality growth**" (churn cut from 95bps to 67bps, service margin 21%→25%). The Security narrative crossed a symbolic line in Q1-2026: "**Security reached cash flow breakeven for the first time in a first quarter**," and Cipher cybersecurity was folded into Security to sell "integrated physical and digital security."

Growth drivers quietly dropped: the old emphasis on top-line CIT volume in Cash is replaced by "transformation products" — a tacit admission that legacy cash logistics is ex-growth and the bet is mix-shift to services. Drivers added: US Security double-digit growth, Alarms unit economics, and balance-sheet deleveraging as the headline value driver.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | The hard evidence | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| "Strong, broad-based growth" | Reported revenue FY2025 +0.5%, Q1-2026 +1.5% (financials/web). 3-yr CAGR +5.7%. Organic +10% / +7.8%. | **Half-true.** Organic strength is genuine; reported is near-zero because FX (ARS/USD) erases it. A shareholder is paid in reported euros, not organic. |
| "On the right track… generating value for shareholders" — earnings inflection | Net income €78M (2024) → €119M (2025), +52.8% (web/financials). EPS €0.15 → €0.22, +46.7% YoY. EBITA €357M, +8.9%. | **TRUE and material.** The load-bearing fact. Earnings power inflects hard while revenue is flat — operating leverage + tax-rate normalization (effective tax 42.0%, −449bps YoY). |
| Margin expansion | Net margin 2.4% (financials); gross 20.4% stable; consolidated EBITDA margin 11.1%, EBITA ~6.8%. Security EBITA margin 2.8%. | **Mixed.** Group margin is structurally thin (manned guarding is a 2–3% EBIT business). Expansion is real at the EBITA line (+8.9%) but off a low base; net margin remains razor-thin. |
| Deleveraging | Net debt €1.62B (financials, 2026-03-31); group reported €1.381B; Net debt/EBITDA 2.4x, −€28M QoQ; target <2.4x by year-end. | **TRUE, slow.** Deleveraging is happening at a modest pace; €1.6B net debt against €119M net income is still heavy leverage. |
| FCF recovery | TTM FCF €218M (financials); P/FCF 5.5x; FCF +53.3% YoY; Q1-2026 operating cash flow +€8M vs −€19M prior-year (web). | **TRUE.** Second load-bearing fact — FCF is real, growing, and 5.5x is genuinely cheap if sustainable. Q1 turning cash-positive is a structural seasonality break. |

Where the story is **richer than the numbers**: "value creation" framing implies a re-rating is owed; nothing in the reported top line (flat) supports a growth re-rate — only the earnings/FCF inflection does. Where it's **poorer**: management undersells how much of the EPS jump is tax-rate + operating leverage rather than demand, and how exposed it is to one more ARS devaluation.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Rising / Stable (confident, not defensive).** Across Q4-2025 and Q1-2026 the tone is offensive: "a strong first quarter," "solid operations performance," "broad-based growth across regions," "on the right track to comply with our main objective." Management volunteers point forward revenue figures (Q2 €1.44B, Q3 €1.49B) — a confidence tell; ex-growth managements give ranges, not points. Language is *operational and granular* (churn bps, transformation-product %, DSO) rather than macro-excuse-laden, a green flag for a LatAm-exposed name. The one defensive note is FX, handled with "robust risk management practices" but no quantified hedge disclosure.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Medium-High.** Financials show beats in 5 of 7 quarters (71%, financials) and FY2025 net profit "exceeding targets" (company headline). EPS delivery compounded +22.4% over 3 years and the +52.8% net-profit jump beat the company's own FY2025 objective. Management gives point revenue forecasts and broadly delivers — Q1-2026 net profit +15.2% continues the streak. The reliability gap is FX: management cannot guide reported numbers credibly (they don't control the peso), so they shifted goalposts to organic + earnings, where they deliver. **Score: Medium-High** on what they control; Low on reported-euro predictability.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

(1) **FX/earnings sensitivity** — pressed for quantified FX impact, management offered "robust risk management practices" with no hedge ratios (a non-answer). (2) **Security margin sustainability** — pushed on whether 2.8% EBITA keeps climbing, the CFO conceded improvements "will be less significant over the course of the year" — the easy margin gains are behind. (3) **Cash/Argentina** — Cash EBITA flat, "negatively impacted by Argentina macroeconomic conditions," the structural soft underbelly. What analysts are NOT pushing on but should: the absence of any catalyst to close the holdco/SoP discount (no buyback ramp, no Cash take-private, no asset sale).

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🟢 Net income +52.8% FY2025, EPS +46.7% — a genuine earnings inflection, not engineering (corroborated by EBITA +8.9% + tax normalization).
🟢 FCF €218M TTM at 5.5x P/FCF; Q1-2026 turned operating-cash-positive (+€8M vs −€19M).
🟢 Alarms unit economics improving (churn 95→67bps, service margin 21%→25%).
🟢 Deleveraging on track (2.4x, −€28M QoQ), BBB rated, 60%+ debt fixed at 2.9% avg cost.
🔴 Net margin 2.4%, Security EBITA margin 2.8%, ROIC 3.7% (financials) — structurally low quality; the business *deserves* a discount.
🔴 Reported revenue flat (+0.5% FY, +1.5% Q1) — the growth story lives in "organic," which FX repeatedly eats.
🔴 €1.6B net debt is heavy; Argentina/ARS is a recurring, uncontrollable hit to Cash.
🔴 **No catalyst.** The only structural event — Gubel's €1.83/share tender — closed March 2024 *below* today's €2.68. No live buyback ramp, no Cash take-private, no asset sale, no activist. Insider buy ratio 0.00 (financials).

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

The terminology shift from "volume" to "**transformation products**" in Cash is the key tell — it concedes legacy CIT is in secular decline and reframes the bet as a services/mix story (margin over volume). The Cipher-into-Security integration signals Prosegur wants to be sold as an *integrated* security platform, not a labor-arbitrage guard company — a multiple-aspiration move, though the 2.8% margin says the market won't pay for it yet. The ritual invocation of "value for shareholders" without any accompanying capital-return action (flat ~€0.13/sh dividend, ~4.9% yield, no buyback acceleration) reads as aspiration, not plan — management knows the stock is cheap and is signaling, but holds no forcing function.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**STRENGTHENING (operationally) / STABLE (structurally).** The operating trajectory is genuinely improving — earnings, FCF, Alarms economics, deleveraging all point the right way, and management is credible and confident on what it controls. But this is a *quality-improving-but-still-low-quality* compounder with **no catalyst to force the discount closed**. The single most important thing the director must weigh: **this is a pure margin-of-safety / FCF-multiple bet, not a special situation.** The €1.83 Gubel tender already fired (below today's price), Gubel already owns ~75%, and there is no announced buyback ramp, Cash take-private, or asset sale to act as a forcing function. You are paid 5.5x FCF and a ~4.9% dividend to wait for a re-rate that has no dated trigger and is perpetually one ARS devaluation away from being erased on the reported line.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 4 | TRAJECTORY: STRENGTHENING

bull_thesis: Prosegur is a physical-security group built on three businesses, and right now all three are improving while the stock trades at just 5.5 times trailing free cash flow (free cash flow is the cash a company generates after paying for the investments it needs to keep running) and pays a dividend of about 4.9%. The case rests on hard results, not a story: net income rose 52.8% in fiscal 2025 (from 78 million euros to 119 million), earnings per share climbed 46.7% (from 0.15 to 0.22 euros), EBITA (operating profit before certain non-cash charges) grew 8.9% to 357 million euros, and trailing free cash flow reached 218 million euros, up 53% from a year earlier. Notably, all of that came despite flat reported revenue (up just 0.5%), which means the gains came from doing more with the same sales plus a normalized tax rate (the effective tax rate was 42.0%, down 449 basis points, or 4.49 percentage points, year over year), not from a temporary demand spike. Reported growth looks weak mainly because the Argentine peso and US dollar shrink the figures when converted into euros; measured in local terms, the business grew about 10% in fiscal 2025 and 7.8% in the first quarter of 2026. Each of the three businesses has its own path to a higher valuation. First, Cash: the roughly 74%-owned and separately listed Prosegur Cash (ticker CASH.MC) is a high-quality cash-in-transit franchise on par with Brink's, earning an 11.3% EBITA margin, with a growing share of recurring 'transformation products' (higher-value, repeat services), now 36.4% of Cash sales; the market already values that unit at 8.9 times EBITDA and a 1.83 billion euro enterprise value (the total value of the business including debt). Second, Security: 2.7 billion euros in revenue, it turned cash-flow positive in a first quarter for the first time, US growth is in double digits, and the Cipher cybersecurity unit has been folded in to sell combined physical and digital protection. Third, Alarms: a genuine subscription compounder, with the customer base up 8.9% to 1.07 million, churn (the rate at which customers cancel) cut from 95 to 67 basis points, service margin up from 21% to 25%, and activation-fee revenue up 35%. The company is also paying down debt steadily (net debt at 2.4 times EBITA, down 28 million euros in the quarter, BBB credit rating, more than 60% of debt fixed at 2.9%), and its guidance is credible (it beat in 5 of the last 7 quarters, beat its own fiscal 2025 target, and gives specific revenue forecasts of 1.44 and 1.49 billion euros for the next two quarters). At 5.5 times free cash flow, you are paid to own a deleveraging, earnings-inflecting cash machine alongside an aligned controlling family. If free cash flow holds at 218 million euros and the market pays even 9 to 10 times it (still a discount to Securitas at roughly 12 to 14 times), the equity would be worth 1.96 to 2.18 billion euros versus about 1.45 billion today, a 35% to 50% re-rating while you collect the roughly 4.9% dividend.
bear_thesis: Prosegur is a low-quality, currency-exposed, thin-margin manned-guarding group that deserves its cheap valuation and has nothing on the horizon to close the gap. Its net margin is just 2.4%, its return on invested capital (the profit it earns on the money put into the business) is 3.7%, and the Security unit's EBITA margin (operating profit before certain non-cash charges, as a share of sales) is only 2.8%. This is a labor-arbitrage guard business with razor-thin economics, and no amount of 'integrated cyber' branding earns it a technology-company valuation on a 2.8% margin. The entire growth story lives in local-currency terms; in reported euros, which is the only currency shareholders are actually paid in, revenue is flat (up 0.5% for the full year, up 1.5% in the first quarter) because the Argentine peso and US dollar keep eroding it. Argentina is a recurring, uncontrollable wound: Cash operating profit was already hurt by Argentine economic conditions, and one more peso devaluation would flatten the reported figures again. The earnings jump is lower-quality than it appears: much of it comes from a normalized tax rate and from operating leverage off a depressed starting point, and management itself conceded that margin gains 'will be less significant over the course of the year.' Debt is heavy at 1.62 billion euros net against just 119 million in net income, and paying it down at roughly 28 million euros a quarter is painfully slow. Most damaging for a deep-value case, there is nothing that forces the value to surface. The only structural event, the Gubel tender offer (a formal bid to buy shares), closed in March 2024 at 1.83 euros per share, below today's 2.68 euros, and the Gubel family already controls about 75%. So the cheap re-rating moment has already come and gone in the wrong direction, and minority shareholders are at the mercy of a controlling family with no obligation to unlock value: no announced buyback ramp, no plan to take Prosegur Cash private, no asset sale, and no activist investor (the ratio of insider buying is 0.00). A listed parent that owns a separately listed subsidiary is a permanent discount machine that rarely narrows without an unexpected corporate action. The stock stays a cheap value trap at 5.5 times free cash flow, and you collect 4.9% to wait indefinitely.
consensus_delta: Here is where we may disagree with the market. For a deeply cheap name like this, the real question is whether investors are pricing in permanent decline when earnings power is actually stable or growing. In this case the market is not badly wrong: it is pricing a deserved quality discount and is roughly right on direction, if not on magnitude. The genuine mispricing the market underweights is the quality of the fiscal 2025 earnings jump (net income up 52.8%, free cash flow up 53%), which is real and durable, driven by operating leverage, a permanently normalized tax rate, and the shift toward subscription revenue in Alarms, not a one-off. Yet the stock still trades at 5.5 times free cash flow as if that cash flow were a temporary peak. The market's error is treating the 218 million euros of group free cash flow as fragile when about 36% of Cash sales are now recurring services and Alarms churn has structurally fallen from 95 to 67 basis points. Where the market is right, and the bullish case has to concede it: reported revenue is flat, the Argentine peso is a recurring drag on the reported line, and there is no catalyst, so the discount can persist for years. The disagreement with the market is modest, not dramatic: a re-rating on improved earnings quality from 5.5 toward 8 times free cash flow, not a special-situation jump all the way to fair value.
forcing_function: There is no hard, dated catalyst to force a re-rating, and that is the central weakness of this case. A catalyst is the specific event that forces the market to revalue a stock; here, none exists. The next concrete events are ordinary earnings reports (the second quarter of 2026, due around July or August, roughly 48 days out per the screener), which can confirm the cash-flow and margin trajectory but are not themselves re-rating events. A true catalyst would require an unannounced corporate action: taking Prosegur Cash private or squeezing out its minority holders (the family already controls the parent, so absorbing the listed subsidiary would collapse the holding-company discount), a sizable buyback authorization, a sale of the Cash or Alarms stake, or an activist investor stepping in, none of which were on the calendar as of June 13, 2026. Without one of those, the re-rating depends on the valuation slowly converging over several quarters. The honest guidance for the director: watch the next one or two reports for clean, currency-adjusted free-cash-flow confirmation and any shift in how management deploys capital (a buyback or a Cash consolidation), and enter on a sell-off, not on a catalyst, because the catalyst does not yet exist.
valley_of_death: The biggest risk over the next three to nine months is an Argentine peso devaluation (and secondarily a dollar move) that flattens the reported results again. Cash operating profit was already hurt by Argentine economic conditions in the first quarter of 2026, and a sharp peso move would erase the reported growth story, and likely the stock's recent strength, even if the local-currency numbers stay fine. The second risk is disappointing Security margins: management has already warned that margin gains 'will be less significant over the course of the year,' so a second- or third-quarter report showing the 2.8% operating margin stalling would puncture the margin-improvement thesis. The third is that debt reduction is slow (about 28 million euros a quarter against 1.62 billion in net debt); any reversal in working capital could stall the deleveraging story that underpins the case (the first-quarter swing to positive operating cash flow of 8 million euros, from negative 19 million a year earlier, is seasonally fragile). There is no wall of maturing debt to refinance (BBB rated, more than 60% fixed, a 60 million euro European Investment Bank loan just secured, and a 600 million euro Cash bond already paid down), so the risk is earnings and currency, not refinancing. There is also no cash-burn hump to clear, since the business generates positive free cash flow.
positioning_washout: There is little forced-selling pressure here, the kind of mechanical selling that can drive a stock down regardless of fundamentals. The dividend (about 0.13 euros per share, a roughly 4.9% yield at a 77% payout) is stable and has not been cut, so there is no trigger for income investors to dump the stock the way they would a dividend-cutter. The more important fact is the opposite of a washout: the Gubel/Revoredo family controls about 75%, so the freely traded shares are a thin and illiquid 25%. That both caps any forced selling and means there is no large mechanical buyer to drive a re-rating. There is no index-inclusion catalyst, no forced-seller setup, and no visible activist accumulation (the ratio of insider buying is 0.00). The thin float and controlled-company structure cut both ways: they put a floor under the downside (no big holder is likely to sell out) but remove any positioning-driven push on the upside.
moderator_conclusion: Prosegur is a high-quality read on a low-quality business: genuinely cheap, but slow-burning, with no catalyst. The forensic review rated its credibility 4 out of 5 with an improving trajectory, since earnings (up 52.8%), free cash flow (up 53%), Alarms economics, and debt reduction are all genuinely getting better, and management is confident and specific about what it controls. Valuing each business line separately (a sum-of-the-parts analysis) brackets the stock between 1.90 euros (strict parts, hurt by currency) and 3.60 euros (a free-cash-flow-anchored bullish view); the reconciled base case is about 2.85 euros, only a thin 6% premium to today's 2.68 euros once the deserved thin-margin discount (2.8% Security operating margin, 3.7% return on invested capital) and the holding-company and currency discounts are respected. The screener's 75% to 81% margin-of-safety estimate (using the Graham and IV15 valuation models) is optimistic. Applying our current approach, which favors hard-dated special situations in overlooked, distressed, deleveraging, or forced-seller corners of the market and explicitly downweights ideas with no dated catalyst or that rely on slow valuation convergence: Prosegur has already used up its only catalyst (the Gubel tender in 2024, at 1.83 euros, below today's price), the family already owns about 75% (thin float, no mechanical buyer to drive a re-rating), and there is no announced buyback, take-private, or asset sale, so on the catalyst dimension this name moves down. The conclusion is to wait, with low conviction (2 out of 5): do not chase it at 2.68 euros. The right entry is a panic sell-off, for example a peso-devaluation scare that knocks the reported revenue and pushes the price toward the bearish 1.90 euros, where you would be buying a deleveraging cash machine generating 218 million euros of free cash flow at about 4 times that cash flow with a roughly 6% yield. Separately, the pure value rating is a 4 out of 5: setting aside the catalyst and the market environment, the value case is strong, a genuinely cheap (5.5 times free cash flow) and genuinely improving (free cash flow and net income both up about 53%) franchise with solid financing (BBB rated) and high forensic credibility (4 out of 5), trading near or below a defensible blended sum-of-the-parts value. The only thing keeping the value rating at 4 rather than 5 is the structurally low returns and margins and the currency fragility, which make part of the discount deserved. The two ratings diverge on purpose: cheap and clean (value 4), but no catalyst and an out-of-favor type of name (conviction 2).
sop_breakdown: The base case blends two ways of valuing the company. The first anchors on consolidated free cash flow, which captures the real 218 million euro cash machine. The second values each business line separately, starting from segment operating profit and subtracting debt, which respects the deserved thin-margin and holding-company discount. We then trim for currency risk and the absence of a catalyst. Valuing the parts at base-case multiples: the 74% Cash stake is worth about 0.78 billion euros (between an intrinsic 0.84 billion and a market 0.72 billion); Security about 0.54 billion (7 times roughly 77 million euros of operating profit); and Alarms about 0.36 billion (1.4 times sales). That gross total of about 1.68 billion, less parent-level net debt of 0.76 billion, leaves 0.92 billion, or roughly 1.70 euros per share on the disaggregated parts. The consolidated free-cash-flow lens (218 million euros at about 8 times equals 1.74 billion euros of equity, or 3.23 euros per share) pulls the value higher, because Prosegur's worth lies in its combined cash generation rather than in separable pieces. Weighting the consolidated cash-machine view at about 60% and the strict parts at about 40%, with a modest currency and holding-company haircut, lands the base-case fair value at 2.85 euros per share, a slim 6% premium to today's 2.68 euros. It is cheap, but the deserved discount eats up most of the headline 75% to 81% model margin of safety.
risk_reward: The margin of safety is thin, and the payoff is not asymmetric. The downside to the bearish sum-of-the-parts value of 1.90 euros is about 29% from today's 2.68 euros; the upside to the base-case fair value of 2.85 euros is about 6%; and the upside to the bullish 3.60 euros is about 34%. That makes the base-case reward-to-risk roughly 0.2 to 1 (poor) and the bull-case reward-to-risk roughly 1.2 to 1. The screener's 75% to 81% margin-of-safety estimate (using the Graham and IV15 valuation models) overstates the edge; once the thin-margin, holding-company, and currency discounts are respected, the realistic upside to fair value is single digits in the base case and about 34% only in a no-currency-shock bullish case. This is a cheap name with a thin margin of safety, not an asymmetric special situation.
catalyst_status: FIRED. The only structural catalyst -- the Gubel, S.L. (Revoredo family) tender offer -- was authorized by CNMV on 2024-02-20 and completed 2024-03-18 at EUR1.83/share for ~13.23% of capital, lifting Gubel to ~74.8% ownership. That event is >2 years old AND priced BELOW today's EUR2.68, so the re-rate it could have driven is spent and went the wrong way for minorities. Verified as of 2026-06-13: NO live tender/squeeze-out, NO announced buyback ramp, NO Prosegur Cash take-private/delisting, NO asset sale, NO activist (insider buy ratio 0.00); the Q1-2026 call disclosed zero capital-action plans beyond routine deleveraging. The thesis rests entirely on FCF-multiple convergence with no dated forcing function.
peer_comps_note: The true comparable companies are global contract-security firms (Securitas, ticker SECU-B.ST, plus G4S and Allied Universal, which are private), along with Prosegur's own listed Cash subsidiary (Prosegur Cash, ticker CASH.MC) and Brink's (ticker BCO) for cash logistics. At 5.5 times price to free cash flow, Prosegur screens cheap against Securitas at roughly 12 to 14 times, but its 3.7% return on invested capital and 2.4% net margin mean the discount is partly earned, reflecting the Latin American currency drag and the structurally low-margin nature of manned guarding. A useful sanity check on the parts valuation: the listed Cash subsidiary trades at 8.9 times EBITDA and a 1.83 billion euro enterprise value (versus Brink's at about 6.5 times), so the crown-jewel segment is not cheap on its own quote; it is the consolidated holding company that carries the discount. Prosegur does look cheap against peers on free cash flow, but once you adjust for quality, the gap to Securitas is justified rather than a pure mispricing.
interrogator_dossier:
# Prosegur Compañía de Seguridad, S.A. (PSG.MC) — A Deep Dive on Management and the Setup

*Prepared with Claude Opus 4.8. Sources: Prosegur's fiscal 2025 results and press release (prosegur.com); the fourth-quarter 2025 and first-quarter 2026 earnings calls and slides (Investing.com transcripts); company investor relations; and CNMV history (the Gubel tender offer). No FMP transcript was bundled, so this note reasons from the screener's financial trajectory cross-referenced against the most recent management commentary found online. Quotes are management phrasings as reported in the first-quarter 2026 and fourth-quarter 2025 call coverage; figures drawn from the screener's data block are labeled (financials). The latest completed quarter is the first quarter of fiscal 2026 (ended March 31, 2026).*

## 1. The narrative arc

Prosegur is a physical-security group built on three businesses: Security (manned guarding plus technology), Cash (cash-in-transit and cash management, held through the roughly 74%-owned, separately listed subsidiary Prosegur Cash, ticker CASH.MC), and Alarms (residential and small-business monitoring). The story management has told over the past several quarters is consistent and, unusually for a deeply cheap name, improving: a multi-year effort to create value for shareholders by paying down debt and recovering margins.

How the story has evolved: the older version was dominated by Latin American currency moves as an existential drag masking real volume growth. By fiscal 2025 the framing shifted to treating local-currency growth as the truth and currency translation as noise. Management now leads every release with local-currency figures (up about 10% in fiscal 2025, up 7.8% in the first quarter of 2026) and treats the reported numbers (up 0.5% for the year, up 1.5% in the quarter) as a translation artifact. The newest thread, introduced in the first quarter of 2026, is structural margin improvement: in Cash, 'transformation products now represent 36.4% of total Cash sales' (recurring, higher-margin services replacing volume-linked cash-in-transit), and in Alarms, a deliberate pivot from growing the customer base at any cost to 'quality growth' (churn cut from 95 to 67 basis points, service margin up from 21% to 25%). The Security story crossed a symbolic line in the first quarter of 2026: 'Security reached cash flow breakeven for the first time in a first quarter,' and the Cipher cybersecurity unit was folded into Security to sell 'integrated physical and digital security.'

Growth drivers that quietly faded: the old emphasis on top-line cash-in-transit volume in Cash has been replaced by 'transformation products,' a tacit admission that legacy cash logistics is no longer growing and that the bet is now on shifting the mix toward services. Drivers added: double-digit US Security growth, Alarms unit economics, and balance-sheet deleveraging as the headline value driver.

## 2. Claims versus the financials

| Management claim | The hard evidence | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| 'Strong, broad-based growth' | Reported revenue up 0.5% in fiscal 2025 and 1.5% in the first quarter of 2026 (financials/web). Three-year compound growth 5.7%. Local-currency growth 10% / 7.8%. | Half-true. The local-currency strength is genuine; reported growth is near zero because the Argentine peso and US dollar erase it. A shareholder is paid in reported euros, not local currency. |
| 'On the right track... generating value for shareholders' (the earnings inflection) | Net income 78 million euros (2024) to 119 million (2025), up 52.8% (web/financials). Earnings per share 0.15 to 0.22 euros, up 46.7% year over year. Operating profit (EBITA) 357 million euros, up 8.9%. | True and material. This is the load-bearing fact. Earnings power inflects hard while revenue is flat, driven by operating leverage and a normalized tax rate (effective rate 42.0%, down 449 basis points, or 4.49 percentage points). |
| Margin expansion | Net margin 2.4% (financials); gross margin 20.4% and stable; consolidated EBITDA margin 11.1%, operating margin about 6.8%. Security operating margin 2.8%. | Mixed. The group margin is structurally thin (manned guarding is a 2% to 3% operating-margin business). Expansion is real at the operating-profit line (up 8.9%) but off a low base; the net margin remains razor-thin. |
| Deleveraging | Net debt 1.62 billion euros (financials, March 31, 2026); group reported 1.381 billion; net debt to operating profit 2.4 times, down 28 million euros in the quarter; target below 2.4 times by year-end. | True, but slow. Debt reduction is happening at a modest pace; 1.6 billion euros of net debt against 119 million of net income is still heavy leverage. |
| Free-cash-flow recovery | Trailing free cash flow 218 million euros (financials); price to free cash flow 5.5 times; free cash flow up 53.3% year over year; first-quarter 2026 operating cash flow positive 8 million euros versus negative 19 million a year earlier (web). | True. This is the second load-bearing fact: free cash flow is real, growing, and 5.5 times is genuinely cheap if sustainable. The first quarter turning cash-positive is a structural seasonality break. |

Where the story is richer than the numbers: the 'value creation' framing implies a re-rating is owed, but nothing in the flat reported top line supports a growth re-rating; only the earnings and free-cash-flow inflection does. Where it is poorer: management undersells how much of the earnings jump comes from the tax rate and operating leverage rather than demand, and how exposed it is to one more peso devaluation.

## 3. Tone and confidence

Rising and stable: confident, not defensive. Across the fourth quarter of 2025 and first quarter of 2026 the tone was on the front foot: 'a strong first quarter,' 'solid operations performance,' 'broad-based growth across regions,' 'on the right track to comply with our main objective.' Management volunteers specific forward revenue figures (1.44 billion euros for the second quarter, 1.49 billion for the third), which is a confidence tell; managements running out of growth give ranges, not specific points. The language is operational and granular (churn in basis points, the transformation-product share, days sales outstanding) rather than full of macro excuses, which is encouraging for a Latin-America-exposed name. The one defensive note is currency, handled with 'robust risk management practices' but no quantified hedge disclosure.

## 4. Guidance credibility

Medium-high. The financials show beats in 5 of 7 quarters (71%, financials) and fiscal 2025 net profit 'exceeding targets' (company headline). Earnings per share compounded at 22.4% over three years, and the 52.8% jump in net profit beat the company's own fiscal 2025 objective. Management gives specific revenue forecasts and broadly delivers; first-quarter 2026 net profit up 15.2% continued the streak. The reliability gap is currency: management cannot credibly guide reported numbers (it does not control the peso), so it shifted the goalposts to local-currency and earnings figures, where it does deliver. The verdict is medium-high on what it controls and low on reported-euro predictability.

## 5. Analyst pressure points

(1) Currency and earnings sensitivity: pressed for a quantified currency impact, management offered 'robust risk management practices' with no hedge ratios, a non-answer. (2) Security margin sustainability: pushed on whether the 2.8% operating margin keeps climbing, the chief financial officer conceded that improvements 'will be less significant over the course of the year,' meaning the easy margin gains are behind it. (3) Cash and Argentina: Cash operating profit was flat, 'negatively impacted by Argentina macroeconomic conditions,' the structural soft underbelly. What analysts are not pushing on but should: the absence of any catalyst to close the holding-company and sum-of-the-parts discount (no buyback ramp, no Cash take-private, no asset sale).

## 6. Negatives and positives

Positives:
- Net income up 52.8% in fiscal 2025 and earnings per share up 46.7%, a genuine earnings inflection rather than financial engineering (corroborated by operating profit up 8.9% plus the tax normalization).
- Free cash flow of 218 million euros over the trailing twelve months at 5.5 times price to free cash flow; the first quarter of 2026 turned operating-cash-positive (8 million euros versus negative 19 million).
- Improving Alarms unit economics (churn from 95 to 67 basis points, service margin from 21% to 25%).
- Debt reduction on track (2.4 times, down 28 million euros in the quarter), BBB rated, more than 60% of debt fixed at a 2.9% average cost.

Negatives:
- Net margin 2.4%, Security operating margin 2.8%, return on invested capital 3.7% (financials): structurally low quality, so the business deserves a discount.
- Reported revenue is flat (up 0.5% for the year, up 1.5% in the quarter); the growth story lives in local-currency terms, which currency repeatedly erases.
- 1.6 billion euros of net debt is heavy, and Argentina and the peso are a recurring, uncontrollable hit to Cash.
- No catalyst. The only structural event, Gubel's tender at 1.83 euros per share, closed in March 2024 below today's 2.68 euros. There is no live buyback ramp, no Cash take-private, no asset sale, and no activist. The ratio of insider buying is 0.00 (financials).

## 7. Hidden signals

The shift in language from 'volume' to 'transformation products' in Cash is the key tell: it concedes that legacy cash-in-transit is in secular decline and reframes the bet as a services and mix story, with margin chosen over volume. Folding Cipher into Security signals that Prosegur wants to be seen as an integrated security platform rather than a labor-arbitrage guard company, a move aimed at a higher valuation, though the 2.8% margin says the market will not pay for it yet. The repeated invocation of 'value for shareholders' without any accompanying capital return (a flat dividend of about 0.13 euros per share, roughly 4.9% yield, no buyback acceleration) reads as aspiration rather than a plan: management knows the stock is cheap and is signaling, but has no event to force the value out.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

Strengthening operationally, stable structurally. The operating trajectory is genuinely improving: earnings, free cash flow, Alarms economics, and debt reduction all point the right way, and management is credible and confident about what it controls. But this is a still-low-quality business that is improving, with no catalyst to force the discount closed. The single most important point for the director to weigh: this is a pure margin-of-safety and free-cash-flow-multiple bet, not a special situation. The 1.83 euro Gubel tender already fired (below today's price), the family already owns about 75%, and there is no announced buyback ramp, Cash take-private, or asset sale to act as a catalyst. You are paid 5.5 times free cash flow and a roughly 4.9% dividend to wait for a re-rating that has no dated trigger and is perpetually one peso devaluation away from being erased on the reported line.

Credibility: 4 out of 5. Trajectory: improving.
